Ruling - ARGUED. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF ARTERRA MISSION BAY, LLC's MOTION FOR JOINDER OF ADDITIONAL PARTIES

Arterra's motion for joinder is DENIED. Arterra fails to show that the Association is a necessary party under CCP 389 such that the court cannot render a fair adjudication in its absence in this indemnity action. Counsel for the Association is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and complies with CRC 3.1312.

Notice Of Motion In Support Of Arterra Mission Bay, Llcs Motion For Preliminary Injunction, Or, In The Alternative, To Stay Arbitration And For Consolidation
Off Calendar Feb-19-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Sean D. Allen, Esq., Lorber greenfield Polito, Telephone 707-290-3625, appearing for Plaintiff Arterra Mission Bay LLC,;
M. Peter Crincella, Fenton Grant et al, Telephone 949-435-3800, Specially appearing for Plaintiff Arterra Mission Bay LLC,;
Jacqueline Forni, Esq., Wood Smith, Henning, et al, Telephone 925-222-3400, appearing for Defendant Lendlease (US); CourtCall

Ruling - ARGUED. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Arterra's motion for preliminary injunction is DENIED.

The Association and JAMS are not parties to this action. In fact, Arterra seeks injunctive relief against non-party JAMS and there is no showing that JAMS even has notice of this motion. While in some circumstances an injunction can run against a non-party agent, the court believes that the better practice would be to name JAMS or at the very least give it notice of this motion. (See Hassell v. Bird (2018) 5 Cal.5th 522, 549 ("Consistent with this principle, courts have long observed a general rule against entering injunctions against nonparties.").)

In addition, Arterra's complaint states no viable claim for injunctive relief. "A preliminary injunction is an interim remedy designed to maintain the status quo pending a decision on the merits. [citation] It is not, in itself, a cause of action. Thus, a cause of action must exist before injunctive relief may be granted. [citation] Accordingly, where the complaint fails to state a cause of action an order granting a preliminary injunction must be reversed. [citation] (MaJor v. Miraverde Homeowners Assn. (1992) 7 Cal.App.4th 618, 623-24.) "A preliminary injunction is warranted only if there is on file a complaint which states a sufficient cause of action for injunctive relief of the character embraced in the preliminary injunction." (Moreno Mutual Irrigation Co. v. Beaumont Irrigation District (1949) 94 Cal.App.2d 766, 778.) In this case, Artrerra is effectively seeking specific performance and/or injunctive relief with respect to Section 5.3.1 of the CC&Rs without pleading such a claim. In Dennis v. Overholtzer (1957) 149 Cal.App.2d 101, the court provides that the trial court can issue an injunction based on the affidavits, but that was in the context of preserving the status quo among parties to the action.

Arterra's alternative motion for a stay or consolidation per CCP 1281.2(c) is DENIED. Arterra fails to show that any of the recently named Does will not agree to arbitration and there exists a possibility of conflicting rulings. (See Bos Material Handling, Inc. v. Crown Controls Corp. (1982) 137 Cal.App.3d 99, 112.) LendLease, a non party to the arbitration agreement, agreed to arbitrate. Counsel for the Association is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and complies with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: W. Trupek; Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR# 8600 email: maria.torreano@gmail.com (302/RBU)
